behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because the failure boundary is the handoff between file discovery, ordering, and execution sequencing (not just a pure sort helper). Integration vs System -> Integration wins because behavior text focuses on ordering logic, and does not require real OS/network/subprocess effects to manifest. Rejection gate: System was considered, but disqualified by behavior text being about version-order sequencing rather than infra-specific failure modes.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner against a test migrations directory containing out-of-order filenames (e.g., 001, 010, 002) with a test DB/repo adapter that records execution order; assert executed order is numeric ascending by prefix.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because correctness depends on state handoff between migration execution and persisted _migrations metadata across runs. Integration vs System -> Integration wins because idempotency can be validated with local module wiring + DB state; no OS/subprocess-specific behavior is required by the behavior text. Rejection gate: System was considered, but disqualified since text asserts skip-on-second-run semantics, not network/process fault behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Execute runner twice on the same test database with one migration that has an observable side effect; assert side effect occurs once and metadata shows a single applied record.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because this is transaction + runner control-flow interaction, not a single-function rule. Integration vs System -> System wins because rollback guarantees depend on real DB transaction semantics; mocks can hide partial-commit failures and statement error behavior. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE both trigger upgrade: real DB behavior is the failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real PostgreSQL instance, run migrations where one file inserts a row then raises SQL error, followed by another migration file; assert inserted row is absent (rolled back) and later migration was not executed.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because failure emerges from cross-instance coordination, beyond one function. Integration vs System -> System wins because behavior explicitly requires concurrent separate processes and real DB locking/uniqueness behavior; in-process fakes can miss race conditions. REAL-INFRA requires System (real concurrency/process + DB interaction).
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes simultaneously against the same real PostgreSQL database and same migration set; after both exit, assert every migration side effect appears exactly once and _migrations has one record per migration.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit vs Integration -> Integration wins because this checks sequencing across schema-initialization and migration-application modules on first-run state. Integration vs System -> Integration wins because required assertion is operation order, which can be caught without OS/subprocess-specific phenomena. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text is about ordering on first run, not infra-level failure conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Run against an empty test database and capture executed DB operations (or observable schema state transitions); assert _migrations creation occurs before first migration execution and run completes with metadata table present.
